turns.content: I'm looking at this open-source project and I thought using std::fs::File with a temporary path would be enough to handle cleanup automatically when the file is closed, so why do I need to import the tempfile crate?

That's a common assumption for new contributors. The problem with std::fs::File on a temporary path is that it doesn't guarantee automatic cleanup; you have to manually call remove_dir_all or file.close() and hope the OS reclaims the space, which can lead to orphaned files if an error occurs before cleanup runs. Instead, crates like tempfile provide NamedTempFile and TempDir types that implement Drop so the filesystem resources are cleaned up immediately when they go out of scope, regardless of errors.
Okay, so I just need to replace my file handling with a NamedTempFile? Does it still give me access to write and read the data like a normal File?

Yes, but think of tempfile as a smart wrapper around std::fs::File that adds safety guarantees. The NamedTempFile type holds a path string internally and delegates all I/O operations to an underlying file descriptor created by the OS, so you can call write and read methods exactly like you would with a regular File, except now the cleanup happens automatically when the variable is dropped.
That makes sense. One thing I'm worried about is if my function panics before writing anything to the temp file, will the directory still be cleaned up?

Exactly right. The why here is about Rust's ownership model and the Drop trait; because NamedTempFile implements Drop, the cleanup code runs whenever the variable leaves scope, even if an earlier panic prevented any writes from happening. This prevents the common bug where a partial write leaves behind empty directories or corrupt files that later cause test failures.
I see. So I should just wrap my temp file creation in try block with NamedTempFile instead of std::fs?

You don't necessarily need a try block if you're using the builder pattern from tempfile, but yes, wrapping your logic ensures safety. Think of it like this: if you were building a temporary house instead of a file, you wouldn't just lay bricks and hope the foundation disappears when construction stops; you'd use a proper site-clearing service that guarantees the land is returned to its original state no matter what happens during construction.
